STOCHASTIC COMPLETENESS FOR LANDMARK SPACE
KAREN HABERMANN AND STEFAN SOMMER
Abstract. We study stochastic completeness for landmark spaces equipped with Riemannian
metrics induced by right-invariant metrics on subgroups of the diffeomorphism group of the
shape domain. We extend a previous stochastic completeness result, which only covers the case
of exactly two landmarks, to landmark spaces with any number of landmarks. This succeeds
the characterization of geodesic completeness for landmark spaces with arbitrary numbers of
landmarks, and thus finishes the completeness characterization for landmark spaces by covering
the stochastic case. The proof makes use of Grigor’yan’s volume growth criterion for stochastic
completeness, which requires a suitable upper bound for the volume of growing geodesic balls.
We obtain quantitative controls for geodesic balls in the landmark space by bounding both its
Euclidean size and the rate at which pairwise landmark distances can approach zero. We then
combine this with a lower bound on the minimal eigenvalue of the landmark cometric in terms of
the Fourier transform of the kernel to yield volume growth bounds sufficient to prove stochastic
completeness of landmark spaces for wide classes of kernels, including Matérn kernels.
1. Introduction
Spaces of landmarks equipped with Riemannian metrics induced from right-invariant metrics on
subgroups of the diffeomorphism group were introduced in [11] and play a central role in shape
analysis, computational anatomy and stochastic models of shape evolution. For configurations of
landmarks in Rd, the metric is encoded by a positive-definite symmetric kernel on Rd. For instance,
for Sobolev metrics on subgroups of the diffeomorphism group, this kernel is of Matérn type. The
resulting Riemannian geometry couples all landmarks and becomes singular only at landmark
collision, so the small-scale behavior of the kernel near the diagonals governs both geodesic and
stochastic completeness properties of the landmark manifold.
For stochastic models of shape evolution, determining stochastic completeness is important to
ensure that Brownian motion exists almost surely for all times, in this case for landmark shape
spaces. In [8], the question concerning long-time existence of Brownian motion was completely
answered for configurations of exactly two landmarks. In particular, for Sobolev metrics one finds
the threshold phenomenon that low-order Sobolev metrics allow landmark collision in finite time
with positive probability, whereas sufficiently regular Sobolev metrics and Gaussian kernels give
long-time existence of Brownian motion. More recently, [10] established a full characterization of
geodesic completeness for landmark spaces with smooth translation- and rotation-invariant metrics
for arbitrary numbers of landmarks, and gave examples of geodesically complete but stochastically
incomplete landmark manifolds.
The purpose of the present paper is to extend the stochastic completeness theory for landmark
spaces from two landmarks to arbitrary numbers of landmarks. For translationally and rotationally
invariant kernels, we link stochastic completeness for any number of landmarks to specific properties
of the Fourier transform of the kernels. This significantly strengthens the results of [8] because
instead of relying on a special reduction available for two landmarks, we give a direct global
argument on the full landmark space. Our proof combines Grigor’yan’s volume growth criterion
2020 Mathematics Subject Classification. 58J65, 62R30, 60J50, 60J70.
Key words and phrases. shape analysis, landmark space, stochastic completeness, Sobolev metric, Matérn kernel.
arXiv:2606.02570v1  [math.PR]  1 Jun 2026
2
K. HABERMANN AND S. SOMMER
with quantitative controls in terms of the Euclidean distance for geodesic balls in the landmark
manifold. We show that in geodesic balls, one can control both the Euclidean size of individual
landmarks and the rate at which pairwise landmark distances may approach zero. We then use a
lower bound on the minimal eigenvalue of the landmark cometric to upper bound the Riemannian
volume growth of geodesic balls.
The total number of landmarks affects the size of the cometric matrix, but the mechanism that
prevents excessive volume growth is still driven by kernel asymptotics when the minimum distance
between landmarks becomes small. We thus show that the threshold for stochastic completeness
observed for Sobolev metrics in the two-landmark case persists for any number of landmarks.
We denote the configuration space of n ≥2 ordered distinct landmarks in Rd, for d ≥1, by
Landn = {x = (x1, . . . , xn) : x1, . . . , xn ∈Rd with xi ̸= xj for i ̸= j},
where xi ∈Rd gives the position of the ith landmark and where xi ̸= xj for i, j ∈{1, . . . , n} with
i ̸= j reflects the condition that the landmarks are mutually distinct. We observe that Landn can
be considered as an open subset of the Euclidean space Rnd.
As derived in [12], the cometric of a Riemannian metric g on Landn induced by a right-invariant
metric on a subgroup of the diffeomorphism group takes the form
(1.1)
gij(x) = K(xi, xj),
for i, j ∈{1, . . . , n} and a positive-definite symmetric kernel K : Rd × Rd →Rd×d. Throughout, we
assume that the kernel K is invariant under both translations and rotations. Translation invariance
implies that K : Rd × Rd →Rd×d only depends on the difference xi −xj of the two inputs xi and
xj whereas rotation invariance means that
K(Axi, Axj) = AK(xi, xj)A−1
for all rotations A ∈SO(d). In particular, while the cometric kernel is in general matrix-valued, a
translationally and rotationally invariant kernel K is uniquely characterized by a continuous radial
scalar function k: [0, ∞) →R such that
(1.2)
K(xi, xj) = k(∥xi −xj∥)Id
in terms of the Euclidean norm ∥· ∥on Rd and the d × d identity matrix Id. We further assume
that k: [0, ∞) →R is positive and strictly decreasing, which for kernels that are positive-definite
in any dimension is guaranteed by [14], also see [18, Proposition 9.14].
Our criterion for long-time existence of Brownian motion on the Riemannian landmark manifold
(Landn, g), that is, for stochastic completeness of (Landn, g), crucially depends on high-frequency
behavior of the Fourier transform on Rd of the function given by x 7→k(∥x∥) for x ∈Rd. Specifically,
if it exists, we write bk: Rd →R for the Fourier transform
bk(ξ) =
Z
Rd e−i⟨x,ξ⟩k(∥x∥) dx.
For kernels that are positive-definite in any dimension, it is a consequence of the characterization
for scalar radial kernels obtained in [14] that ∥ξ∥7→bk(∥ξ∥) is a decreasing function on (0, ∞).
We establish the following broad criterion for stochastic completeness of the landmark manifold
(Landn, g) for all n ≥2 and d ≥1 in terms of the cometric kernel. While the landmark configuration
space Landn is not connected for d = 1, each connected component is stochastically complete under
the assumed conditions, and thus, Brownian motion started in one connected component remains
in that component for all times.
STOCHASTIC COMPLETENESS FOR LANDMARK SPACE
3
Theorem 1.1. Suppose that the radial scalar function k: [0, ∞) →R is smooth on (0, ∞) and that
it possesses a positive continuous Fourier transform bk: Rd →R. In addition, assume that there
exist ε > 0 and constants b, c > 0 as well as p ≥1 such that, for all r ∈(0, ε),
k(0) −k(r) ≤−cr2 log(r)
and, for all sufficiently high frequencies ξ ∈Rd,
bk(ξ) ≥b∥ξ∥−p.
Then the landmark manifold (Landn, g) is stochastically complete for all n ≥2.
We remark that the high-frequency decay control for the Fourier transform bk prevents the even
extension to R of the radial scalar function k: [0, ∞) →R to be smooth at zero.
The assumed condition that k(0) −k(r) ≤−cr2 log(r) near zero is needed to control the rate at
which pairwise distances between distinct landmarks may approach zero. At the same time, as a
bound polynomial in r and log(r), it cannot be relaxed for a broad stochastic completeness criterion
because the geodesically complete yet stochastically incomplete landmark manifolds constructed
in [10] use kernels that near zero satisfy k(0) −k(r) = r2(1 −log(r))β for β ∈(1, 2]. However, it
is instead possible to assume an alternative regularity condition on the even extension to R of the
radial scalar function k, which results in a slightly weaker criterion for stochastic completeness.
Corollary 1.2. Suppose that the radial scalar function k: [0, ∞) →R is smooth on (0, ∞) and
that its even extension to R is twice continuously differentiable at zero. In addition, assume that k
possesses a positive continuous Fourier transform bk: Rd →R and that there exist p ≥1 as well as
a constant b > 0 such that, for all sufficiently high frequencies ξ ∈Rd,
bk(ξ) ≥b∥ξ∥−p.
Then the landmark manifold (Landn, g) is stochastically complete for all n ≥2.
An important class of translationally and rotationally invariant kernels K : Rd × Rd →Rd×d
used in applications arises from Sobolev operators, in the sense that the integral operator with
kernel K is the inverse of the Sobolev differential operator LHs = (Id −σ2∆)s for s > d
2 and σ > 0.
The associated radial scalar function is the Matérn kernel given by, with ν = s −d
2 > 0,
k(r) = Cd,s,σ
 r
σ
ν
Kν
 r
σ

,
where Kν denotes the modified Bessel function of the second kind and Cd,s,σ > 0 is a normalizing
constant. More details are provided and discussed in Section 2. When applied specifically to the
Matérn kernels, Theorem 1.1 gives the following characterization, which yields the same threshold
previously observed in [8] for the two-landmark case.
Corollary 1.3. Suppose the Riemannian metric g on the landmark configuration space Landn
arises from the Sobolev operator LHs. If we have s ≥1 + d
2 then the landmark manifold (Landn, g)
is stochastically complete for all n ≥2.
We will see that for the critical case ν = s−d
2 = 1, we are exactly in a situation where k(0)−k(r)
behaves like −cr2 log(r) near zero and where Corollary 1.2 would not apply. We further remark
that for ν ∈(0, 1), the criterion derived in [8] shows that on the landmark space (Landn, g) with
n = 2, the two landmarks collide with positive probability in finite time.
This threshold observed in the stochastic completeness for cometric kernels arising from Sobolev
operators is exactly the same one as for the geodesic completeness, where it is a consequence of [2]
or [10] that, for any number of landmarks, the manifold (Landn, g) is geodesically complete if and
only if s ≥1 + d
2. On the level of the infinite-dimensional Sobolev diffeomorphism groups, geodesic
4
K. HABERMANN AND S. SOMMER
completeness is known for s > 1 + d
2, see [3, 5], with very limited results available in the critical
case s = 1 + d
2 such as [4, 13].
In the proof of Theorem 1.1, we make use of the following result by Grigor’yan, see [6, Theorem 1]
and [7, Theorem 9.1], which states that a geodesically complete Riemannian manifold subject to a
suitable volume growth control for growing geodesic balls is also stochastically complete.
Theorem 1.4 (Grigor’yan [6, 7]). Let (M, g) be a geodesically complete Riemannian manifold.
Suppose that, for some z ∈M, the volumes V (r) of the geodesic balls with center z and of radius
r > 0 satisfy, for some a > 0,
Z ∞
a
r dr
log V (r) = ∞.
Then (M, g) is stochastically complete.
The criterion by Grigor’yan was successfully applied in [9] for spaces of discrete regular curves
to prove that also there sufficiently regular Sobolev-type metrics ensure stochastic completeness.
As detailed later, under the conditions imposed in Theorem 1.1, the geodesic completeness of the
landmark manifold (Landn, g) is implied by the characterization derived in [10] that the landmark
manifold (Landn, g) is geodesically complete if and only if, for all a > 0,
Z a
0
dr
p
k(0) −k(r)
= ∞.
The core of the argument therefore lies in obtaining a sufficiently good volume growth control
for growing geodesic balls that allows us to apply the criterion by Grigor’yan. This is achieved
by controlling both the Euclidean norm of individual landmarks and their pairwise distances on
geodesic balls, and by lower bounding the minimal eigenvalue of the cometric of the Riemannian
metric g in terms of the minimal pairwise landmark distance.
Paper outline. In Section 2, we first present an overview of cometric kernels and their Fourier
transforms, with a focus on Matérn kernels arising from Sobolev differential operators. We then
proceed in Section 3 to derive the required controls for Euclidean norms and pairwise distances on
geodesic balls, with Lemma 3.1 providing an upper bound for the Euclidean norm of individual
landmarks and Lemma 3.2 giving a general lower bound for the pairwise distances of landmarks.
The explicit lower bound obtained for the minimal pairwise distance under the assumed behavior
of the kernel near zero is stated in Lemma 3.4. In Section 4, we establish an upper bound for
the volume form on geodesic balls by exploiting a lower bound on the minimal eigenvalue of the
cometric in terms of the minimal pairwise distance. We finally put everything together in Section 5
to prove Theorem 1.1, Corollary 1.2 and Corollary 1.3.
Acknowledgements. The work presented in this paper was supported by the Villum Foundation
Grant 40582, the Novo Nordisk Foundation grants NNF18OC0052000, NNF24OC0093490 and
NNF24OC0089608, and by the London Mathematical Society through a Computer Science Small
Grant (Scheme 7).
2. Cometric kernels and their Fourier transforms
We give a more detailed overview of cometric kernels on shape spaces and discuss properties of
their Fourier transforms, with a focus on the Matérn kernels that arise from Sobolev differential
operators.
The large deformation diffeomorphic metric mapping framework employed in shape analysis
characterizes shape variations as diffeomorphic deformations of the ambient space in which the
shapes reside, see, e.g., [15, 17, 18]. It uses a right-invariant Riemannian metric on a suitable
subgroup of the diffeomorphism group of the shape domain to quantify shape changes. A frequently
STOCHASTIC COMPLETENESS FOR LANDMARK SPACE
5
considered subgroup of the diffeomorphism group Diff(Rd) for Rd is the infinite-dimensional Lie
group Diffc(Rd) of compactly supported diffeomorphisms. The right-invariant Riemannian metric
then arises by extending an inner product for which point evaluations are norm-continuous on the
space Xc(Rd) of compactly supported vector fields on Rd. The completion of Xc(Rd) with respect
to this inner product is a Hilbert space with a positive reproducing kernel K : Rd × Rd →Rd×d.
The metric on the subgroup Diffc(Rd) descends to the landmark configuration space Landn due
to the assumed right-invariance. A compactly supported diffeomorphism ϕ ∈Diffc(Rd) acts on a
landmark configuration x ∈Landn from the left as ϕ.x = (ϕ(x1), . . . , ϕ(xn)). For x ∈Landn fixed,
this action yields a submersion from Diffc(Rd) to Landn. The right-invariance of the metric on
Diffc(Rd) then implies that there exists a unique Riemannian metric g on Landn such that the
submersion from Diffc(Rd) to Landn is Riemannian, which is exactly the metric whose cometric is
given by (1.1), see [12] for further details.
If the inner product on Xc(Rd) is induced by the Sobolev differential operator
LHs =
␀Id −σ2∆
s
for s > d
2 and σ > 0, we determine the positive reproducing kernel K, whose associated integral
operator is the inverse of LHs, by working in the Fourier domain.
For a function f : Rd →R, its Fourier transform bf : Rd →R on Rd, if it exists, is defined by, for
ξ ∈Rd,
bf(ξ) =
Z
Rd e−i⟨x,ξ⟩f(x) dx,
with the inverse given by, for x ∈Rd,
f(x) =
1
(2π)d
Z
Rd ei⟨x,ξ⟩bf(ξ) dξ.
The Sobolev differential operator LHs now has the Fourier symbol, for ξ ∈Rd,
\
LHsv(ξ) =
␀1 + σ2∥ξ∥2s bv(ξ)
applied componentwise to vector fields. The corresponding Green’s kernel is therefore scalar and
invariant under both translations and rotations. Since its Fourier transform bk: Rd →R on Rd has
the form, for ξ ∈Rd,
(2.1)
bk(ξ) =
␀1 + σ2∥ξ∥2−s ,
computing the inverse Fourier transform shows that the associated radial scalar function is indeed
the Matérn kernel, with r = ∥x∥and ν = s −d
2 > 0,
k(r) = Cd,s,σ
 r
σ
ν
Kν
 r
σ

.
For Matérn kernels, the decay of bk at high frequency is of order ∥ξ∥−2s, while the behavior of the
radial scalar function k near zero is encoded by the modified Bessel function Kν of the second
kind. We have the asymptotics, as r ↓0,
(2.2)
rνKν(r) = 2ν−1Γ(ν) −





−2ν−1Γ(−ν)r2ν + o
␀r2ν
,
ν ∈(0, 1) ,
−2−1r2 log(r) + o
␀r2 log(r)

,
ν = 1 ,
2ν−3Γ(ν −1)r2 + o
␀r2
,
ν ∈(1, ∞) ,
which follow from [1, Equations (6.1.15-17) and (9.6.2–11)].

3. Landmark norms and pairwise distances on geodesic balls
We prove an upper bound for the Euclidean norm of individual landmarks and a lower bound for
the pairwise distances of landmarks on geodesic balls in the landmark manifold. In particular, we
will see that geodesic balls of growing radii neither grow too rapidly nor approach the collision set
too rapidly if measured with respect to the Euclidean metric.
A key relation used in proving these bounds is that, for a landmark configuration x ∈Landn, a
vector v ∈TxLandn and a covector α ∈T ⋆
xLandn, we have that
(3.1)
α(v)2 ≤g(v, v) g−1(α, α).
This is true in a general Riemannian manifold and follows from the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality
after applying the musical isomorphism sharp induced by g. The inequality (3.1) is also exploited
in [10] to bound the lengths of curves on landmark spaces in terms of integrals involving the radial
scalar function k.
We denote the open geodesic ball in the landmark manifold (Landn, g) with center z ∈Landn
and of radius R > 0 by B(z, R), which is the set of all x ∈Landn that can be connected to z
through a curve of length less than R. The following lemma provides a control for the Euclidean
norm of individual landmarks in a landmark configuration x ∈B(z, R). Throughout, we have
x = (x1, . . . , xn) and z = (z1, . . . , zn) if we need to refer to individual landmarks in Rd.
Lemma 3.1. Suppose the radial scalar function k: [0, ∞) →R is smooth on (0, ∞). Fix z ∈Landn
and let R > 0. We then have, for all x ∈B(z, R) and all i ∈{1, . . . , n},
∥xi∥≤
p
k(0)R + ∥zi∥.
Proof. For δ > 0 and i ∈{1, . . . , n}, we define f δ
i : Landn →R by
f δ
i (x) =
p
∥xi∥2 + δ.
Using that the cometric is given by (1.1) and (1.2), and that, for all x ∈Landn,
␀df δ
i

(x) = ⟨xi, dxi⟩
f δ
i (x) ,
we obtain
g−1(x)
␀df δ
i , df δ
i

=
1
␀f δ
i (x)
2
␀k(0)∥xi∥2
.
Since δ > 0, it follows that
(3.2)
0 < g−1 ␀df δ

i

< k(0).
In the next step, we use that due to x ∈B(z, R) there exists a piecewise differentiable curve
γ : [0, T] →Landn for some T > 0 such that γ(0) = z and γ(T) = x of length less than R, that is,
(3.3)
Z T

where we write ˙γ = dγ

Note that the latter indeed remains true even if f δ
i (x) < f δ
i (z). As δ > 0 was arbitrary, the claimed
result follows from (3.3).
□
We proceed by analyzing pairwise distances between landmarks in landmark configurations on
geodesic balls. The following general result is subsequently used to derive an explicit lower bound for
the pairwise distances of landmarks under the additional assumption that k(0)−k(r) ≤−cr2 log(r)
near zero.
Lemma 3.2. Suppose the radial scalar function k: [0, ∞) →R is smooth on (0, ∞). Fix z ∈Landn
and let R > 0. We then have, for all x ∈B(z, R) and all i, j ∈{1, . . . , n} with i ̸= j,
Z ∥zi−zj∥
∥xi−xj∥
dr
p
2 (k(0) −k(r))
< R.
Proof. We let fij : Landn →R, for i, j ∈{1, . . . , n} with i ̸= j, be given by
fij(x) = ∥xi −xj∥.
For all x ∈Landn, we have fij(x) ̸= 0 and
(dfij) (x) = ⟨xi −xj, dxi −dxj⟩
fij(x)
.
Together with (1.1) and (1.2), we obtain
g−1(x)(dfij, dfij) =
1
(fij(x))2
␀k(0)∥xi −xj∥2 −2k(∥xi −xj∥)∥xi −xj∥2 + k(0)∥xi −xj∥2
,
that is,
(3.4)
g−1(x)(dfij, dfij) = 2 (k(0) −k (fij(x))) .
Since x ∈B(z, R), there exists a piecewise differentiable curve γ : [0, T] →Landn for some T > 0
such that γ(0) = x and γ(T) = z of length less than R. We write ˙γ = dγ
dt . From (3.1) and (3.4), it
follows that, for almost all t ∈[0, T],
 d
dt (fij ◦γ) (t)

p
2 (k(0) −k (fij (γ(t))))
≤
p
g (˙γ(t), ˙γ(t)).
Integrating this inequality from 0 to T and using the change of variables r = fij(γ(t)), we deduce
Z ∥zi−zj∥
∥xi−xj∥
dr
p
2 (k(0) −k(r))

Z T
0

2 (k(0) −k (fij (γ(t))))
dt ≤

p
g (˙γ(t), ˙γ(t)) dt < R,
as required.
□
Remark 3.3. We note that in Lemma 3.2, it is not necessary to assume geodesic completeness of
the landmark manifold (Landn, g). According to [10], under translational and rotational invariance,
geodesic completeness is equivalent to, for a > 0,
Z a
0
dr
p
k(0) −k(r)
= ∞.
In particular, on a geodesically incomplete landmark manifold and for sufficiently large R > 0, the
inequality stated in Lemma 3.2 is automatically true whenever ∥xi −xj∥≤∥zi −zj∥, even when
the distance ∥xi −xj∥becomes arbitrarily small.
At the same time, for a geodesically complete landmark manifold, it is exactly Lemma 3.2
that allows us to derive a lower bound for the pairwise distances between landmarks in landmark
configurations on geodesic balls.
8
K. HABERMANN AND S. SOMMER
Following [16], we denote the separation distance of a landmark configuration x ∈Landn by
q(x), which is defined by
q(x) = 1
2 min
i̸=j ∥xi −xj∥.
It gives the maximum radius r > 0 such that the open Euclidean balls in Rd of radius r and
centered at the individual landmarks are all disjoint.
The next lemma provides a lower bound for the separation distance of landmark configurations
on geodesic balls under the assumption that k(0) −k(r) ≤−cr2 log(r) near zero, which is one of
the assumptions in Theorem 1.1.
Lemma 3.4. Suppose that the radial scalar function k: [0, ∞) →R is smooth on (0, ∞) and that
there exist ε > 0 as well as a constant c > 0 such that k(0) −k(r) ≤−cr2 log(r) for all r ∈(0, ε).
Fix z ∈Landn. For sufficiently large R > 0, we then have, for all x ∈B(z, R),
q(x) ≥1
2 exp
␀−2cR2
.
Proof. By definition of the separation distance, we need to show that, for sufficiently large R > 0,
we have, for all x ∈B(z, R) and for all i, j ∈{1, . . . , n} with i ̸= j,
(3.5)
∥xi −xj∥≥exp
␀−2cR2
.
If ∥xi −xj∥≥ε this is certainly true for sufficiently large R.
We now consider the case where ∥xi −xj∥< ε for x ∈B(z, R) and i, j ∈{1, . . . , n} with i ̸= j.
Under the assumptions on the radial scalar function k we have, for all r ∈(0, ε),
(3.6)
1
p
−2cr2 log(r)
≤
1
p
2(k(0) −k(r))
.
The left-hand side of the inequality is well-defined because log(r) < 0 for r ∈(0, 1) and the bound
on the radial scalar function k can only be true for some ε ∈(0, 1). We compute, for a ∈(0, ε),
Z ε
a
dr
p
−2cr2 log(r)
=
r
2
c
p
−log(a) −
p
−log(ε)

,
which together with (3.6) implies that, for a ∈(0, ε),
(3.7)
r
2

p
−log(a) −
p
−log(ε)

≤
Z ε
a
dr
p
2(k(0) −k(r))
.
Set
(3.8)
Cij =
Z ∥zi−zj∥
ε
dr
p
2(k(0) −k(r))
−
r
2
c
p
−log(ε).
It is important to note that the constant Cij may be negative. By Lemma 3.2, we have
Z ε
∥xi−xj∥
dr
p
2 (k(0) −k(r))
+
Z ∥zi−zj∥
ε
dr
p
2 (k(0) −k(r))
=
Z ∥zi−zj∥
∥xi−xj∥
dr
p
2 (k(0) −k(r))
≤R.
Using (3.7) with a = ∥xi −xj∥as well as (3.8), we obtain
r
2
c
q
−log (∥xi −xj∥) + Cij ≤R.
If we now choose R > 0 large enough such that R > −Cij then
r
2
c
q
−log (∥xi −xj∥) ≤2R.
STOCHASTIC COMPLETENESS FOR LANDMARK SPACE
9
As the left-hand side of the latest inequality is guaranteed to be positive, we can square both sides
to deduce
−log (∥xi −xj∥) ≤2cR2,
which in turn shows that
∥xi −xj∥≥exp
␀−2cR2
.
Since there are only finitely many i, j ∈{1, . . . , n} with i ̸= j, we can thus indeed choose
R > 0 large enough such that (3.5) is true for all non-trivial pairwise distances between landmarks,
thereby proving the claimed result.
□
4. Control for volume form on geodesic balls
We derive an upper bound for the Riemannian volume form on geodesic balls in the landmark
manifold, which subsequently yields a sufficiently good upper bound for the volume of geodesic
balls. The control for the volume form is obtained by lower bounding the minimal eigenvalue of
the landmark cometric in terms of the separation distance of a landmark configuration.
We first observe that, with the assumed translation and rotation invariance, the cometric given
by (1.1) and (1.2) on Landn has, for a fixed radial scalar function k: [0, ∞) →R, the same
eigenvalues for all d ≥1, only their multiplicity changes accordingly. Since for d = 1 the cometric
of the Riemannian metric g on Landn has the same form as the interpolation matrix considered
by Wendland in [16], the following theorem is a consequence of [16, Theorem 12.3].
Theorem 4.1. Suppose that the radial scalar function k: [0, ∞) →R is smooth on (0, ∞) and
that it possesses a positive continuous Fourier transform bk: Rd →R. For M > 0, we set
bkM :=
inf
∥ξ∥≤2M
bk(ξ).
Fix a landmark configuration x ∈Landn. Then, for any
M ≥
12
q(x)
 
π
␀Γ
␀1 + d
2
2
9
!
1
1+d
or, simply,
M ≥
7d
q(x),
the minimal eigenvalue λmin
␀g−1(x)

of the cometric at the landmark configuration x satisfies
λmin
␀g−1(x)

≥
bkM
2Γ
␀1 + d
2

 M
23/2
d
.
The Riemannian volume form volg on the landmark manifold (Landn, g) can be expressed in
terms of the Euclidean volume form dω on Rnd as
(4.1)
volg =
p
det g dω.
The following result therefore provides an upper bound for the volume form on geodesic balls in
the landmark manifold. It is a consequence of the explicit lower bound for the separation distance
of landmark configurations in geodesic balls stated in Lemma 3.4 and the lower bound on the
minimal eigenvalue of the landmark cometric given by Theorem 4.1.
Lemma 4.2. Suppose that the radial scalar function k: [0, ∞) →R is smooth on (0, ∞) and that
it possesses a positive continuous Fourier transform bk: Rd →R. In addition, assume that there
exist ε > 0 and constants b, c > 0 as well as p ≥1 such that, for all r ∈(0, ε),
k(0) −k(r) ≤−cr2 log(r)
10
K. HABERMANN AND S. SOMMER
and, for all sufficiently high frequencies ξ ∈Rd,
bk(ξ) ≥b∥ξ∥−p.
Fix z ∈Landn. Then there exist constants Cd, cd > 0 depending on d ≥1 and the regularity of the
function k such that, for sufficiently large R > 0, we have, for all x ∈B(z, R),
p
det g(x) ≤Cd exp
␀ncdR2
.
Proof. By the assumptions on the Fourier transform bk: Rd →R, there exist p ≥1 and a constant
b > 0 such that, for sufficiently large M > 0,
(4.2)
bkM =
inf
∥ξ∥≤2M
bk(ξ) ≥b(2M)−p.
Without loss of generality, we may and will suppose that p ≥1 + d. By Lemma 3.4, for sufficiently
large R > 0, we further have that, for all x ∈B(z, R),
q(x) ≥1
2 exp
␀−2cR2
.
Since this implies that, for all x ∈B(z, R),
7d
q(x) ≤14d exp
␀2cR2
,
we can apply Theorem 4.1 with M = 14d exp
␀2cR2
. Due to (4.2), this shows that there exists a
constant Bd > 0 depending on b > 0, d ≥1 and p ≥d + 1 such that, for sufficiently large R > 0,
we have, for all x ∈B(z, R),
λmin
␀g−1(x)

≥b (2M)−p
2Γ
␀1 + d
2

 M
23/2
d
= Bd exp
␀−2c(p −d)R2
,
where p −d ≥1 by construction. We finally use the relation
det g(x) ≤
␀λmin
␀g−1(x)
−nd
to deduce the claimed result.
□
5. Stochastic completeness arguments
We conclude by combining our results in previous sections to prove Theorem 1.1 and then arguing
how Corollary 1.2 and Corollary 1.3 follow.
We establish Theorem 1.1 by using the upper bound for the Euclidean norm of individual
landmarks in landmark configurations on geodesic balls, see Lemma 3.1, and the upper bound for
the volume form on geodesic balls, see Lemma 4.2, and by applying Grigor’yan’s volume growth
criterion stated in Theorem 1.4.
Proof of Theorem 1.1. By assumption, we have k(0) −k(r) ≤−cr2 log(r) for all r ∈(0, ε), which
implies that, for all a ∈(0, ε),
Z a
0
dr
p
k(0) −k(r)
≥
Z a
0
dr
p
−cr2 log(r)
= lim
r↓0
2
√c
p
−log(r) −
p
−log(a)

= ∞.
Thus, by the criterion derived in [10] and stated in the introduction, the landmark manifold
(Landn, g) is geodesically complete.
Now fix z ∈Landn. By Lemma 3.1, for sufficiently large R > 0, we then have, for all x ∈B(z, R)
and all i ∈{1, . . . , n},
(5.1)
∥xi∥≤2
p
k(0)R.
STOCHASTIC COMPLETENESS FOR LANDMARK SPACE
11
Let V (z, R) denote the volume of the geodesic ball B(z, R) with respect to the Riemannian volume
form volg on (Landn, g) given by (4.1). From Lemma 4.2 and the spherical bound (5.1), we deduce
that there exist constants Ad, cd > 0 depending on d ≥1 and properties of the function k such
that, for all sufficiently large R > 0,
V (z, R) ≤AdRnd exp
␀ncdR2
.
It follows that, for sufficiently large a > 0, we have
Z ∞
a
R dR
log V (z, R) ≥
Z ∞
a
R dR
log(Ad) + nd log(R) + ncdR2 = ∞.
Therefore, the volume growth criterion by Grigor’yan shows that the landmark manifold (Landn, g)
is stochastically complete for all n ≥2.
□
We see that Corollary 1.2 is a consequence of Theorem 1.1 because if the even extension to R
of k: [0, ∞) →R is twice continuously differentiable at zero then, for some constant c > 0 and as
r ↓0,
k(r) = k(0) −1
2cr2 + o
␀r2
≥k(0) + 1
2cr2 log(r) + o
␀r2
,
and so all the assumptions in Theorem 1.1 are satisfied.
Moreover, Corollary 1.3 follows from Theorem 1.1 due to the small-scale asymptotics for the
Matérn kernels being governed by (2.2) with ν = s −d
2 ≥1 and the property of the positive
continuous Fourier transform given in (2.1) that the decay at high frequency is of order ∥ξ∥−2s.
We close by highlighting that for s = 1 + d
2, that is, with ν = 1, we are exactly in the small-scale
regime where, for some constant c > 0 and as r ↓0,
k(r) = k(0) + 1
2cr2 log(r) + o
␀r2 log(r)

,
that is, where the full force of Theorem 1.1 is needed.
References
[1] Milton Abramowitz and Irene A. Stegun. Handbook of Mathematical Functions with Formulas, Graphs, and
Mathematical Tables. National Bureau of Standards Applied Mathematics Series 55. Tenth Printing. ERIC,
1972.
[2] Martin Bauer, Martins Bruveris, and Peter W. Michor. Overview of the geometries of shape spaces and diffeo-
morphism groups. Journal of Mathematical Imaging and Vision, 50(1-2):60–97, 2014.
[3] Martin Bauer, Joachim Escher, and Boris Kolev. Local and global well-posedness of the fractional order EPDiff
equation on Rd. Journal of Differential Equations, 258(6):2010–2053, 2015.
[4] Martin Bauer, Boris Kolev, and Stephen C. Preston. Geodesic completeness of the H3/2 metric on Diff(S1).
Monatshefte für Mathematik, 193(2):233–245, 2020.
[5] Martins Bruveris and François-Xavier Vialard. On completeness of groups of diffeomorphisms. Journal of the
European Mathematical Society, 19(5):1507–1544, 2017.
[6] Alexander Grigor’yan. Stochastically complete manifolds. Doklady Akademii Nauk SSSR, 290(3):534–537, 1986.
[7] Alexander Grigor’yan. Analytic and geometric background of recurrence and non-explosion of the Brownian
motion on Riemannian manifolds. Bulletin (New Series) of the American Mathematical Society, 36(2):135–249,
1999.
[8] Karen Habermann, Philipp Harms, and Stefan Sommer. Long-time existence of Brownian motion on configu-
rations of two landmarks. Bulletin of the London Mathematical Society, 56(5):1658–1679, 2024.
[9] Karen Habermann and Emmanuel Hartman. Brownian motion on spaces of discrete regular curves. Electronic
Communications in Probability, 31:1–11, Article No. 20, 2026.
[10] Karen Habermann, Stephen C. Preston, and Stefan Sommer. Characterization of geodesic completeness for
landmark space. Discrete and Continuous Dynamical Systems, 50:224–240, 2026.
[11] Sarang C. Joshi and Michael I. Miller. Landmark matching via large deformation diffeomorphisms. IEEE
Transactions on Image Processing, 9(8):1357–1370, 2000.

K. HABERMANN AND S. SOMMER
[12] Mario Micheli, Peter W. Michor, and David Mumford. Sectional curvature in terms of the cometric, with
applications to the Riemannian manifolds of landmarks. SIAM Journal on Imaging Sciences, 5(1):394–433,
2012.
[13] Stephen C. Preston and Pearce Washabaugh. Euler–Arnold equations and Teichmüller theory. Differential
Geometry and its Applications, 59:1–11, 2018.
[14] Isaac J. Schoenberg. Metric spaces and completely monotone functions. Annals of Mathematics. Second Series,
39(4):811–841, 1938.

Computer Vision, 28(3):213–221, 1998.

putational Mathematics. Cambridge University Press, Cambridge, 2005.

58(2):565–586, 1998.
